prompt:
Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A school district spends $2 million annually on a remedial math program
for students who score below grade level on standardized tests. The
program shows that 60% of enrolled students improve their scores the
following year. The district considers this a success and wants to
expand the program. Before expanding, what critical comparison is
missing from this evaluation? What statistical phenomenon might explain
the 60% improvement rate even if the program had zero effect?

parsed.strategy: Identify confounds and baseline comparisons — ask 'compared to what?'
parsed.hardest_part: Recognizing that an impressive-sounding 60% figure is meaningless without a counterfactual, and naming regression to the mean precisely
parsed.solution:
The critical missing comparison is a control group: what percentage of similar below-grade-level students improved WITHOUT the program? If 55% of untreated comparable students also improve naturally, the program's net effect is only 5 percentage points — not 60%. The district is measuring absolute improvement, not attributable improvement.

The statistical phenomenon is regression to the mean. Students are selected precisely because they scored unusually low. Extreme scores on any noisy measurement (standardized tests) contain a luck/error component. On retesting, those students will tend to score closer to their true ability — higher than their initial outlier score — regardless of any intervention. A program that enrolls the lowest scorers will always appear to 'work' by this mechanism alone, even if it does nothing. The 60% improvement rate could be entirely or largely explained by this artifact.
